testCases.title: Verify that the /api/deserialize endpoint accepts only well-formed JSON and rejects the legacy gadget-channel (octet-stream) input
testCases.owasp: A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures
testCases.description: The /api/deserialize endpoint previously used unsafe native Java deserialisation (ObjectInputStream.readObject) which was vulnerable to ysoserial gadget-chain RCE. The fix replaces the native deserialiser with a strict JSON parser (Jackson, fail-on-unknown-properties), and binds the controller to application/json only. The endpoint now echoes back the parsed type name and the field count so the lab's API shape is preserved. This contract verifies the post-remediation behaviour: well-formed JSON is accepted, octet-stream (the legacy gadget channel) is rejected at the media-type gate, and malformed JSON is rejected by the parser.
preconditions: Application is running and the /api/deserialize endpoint is reachable.
An HTTP Basic credential with access to the /api/deserialize endpoint (e.g. alice/alice123).
No ysoserial or Java serialisation tooling is required for the post-remediation contract.
testSteps: Step 1: Send a POST request to /api/deserialize with a well-formed JSON object body (Content-Type: application/json) and confirm the server responds with HTTP 200.
Step 2: Send a POST request to /api/deserialize with Content-Type: application/octet-stream and a non-empty body and confirm the server responds with HTTP 415 (legacy gadget channel is closed).
Step 3: Send a POST request to /api/deserialize with Content-Type: application/json and a malformed JSON body and confirm the server responds with HTTP 400 (strict parser rejects invalid input).
testCases.expectedResult: Well-formed JSON (Content-Type: application/json) is accepted with HTTP 200 and an echo response describing the parsed type and size. Content-Type: application/octet-stream is rejected with HTTP 415, proving the legacy Java-serialisation channel is closed at the framework boundary and no ObjectInputStream readObject() is ever invoked. Malformed JSON is rejected with HTTP 400 by the strict Jackson parser.
references: OWASP Top 10 2021 - A08: Software and Data Integrity Failures

testCases.title: Verify that /api/comment/greet HTML-escapes the 'name' query parameter to prevent reflected XSS
testCases.description: The /api/comment/greet endpoint previously concatenated the 'name' query parameter directly into an HTML response, allowing a reflected XSS payload such as <script>alert('XSS')</script> to execute in the victim's browser. The fix HTML-escapes the five special characters (&, <, >, ", ') before interpolating the parameter, so any HTML/JS payload is rendered as inert text. This test verifies that the escaping is applied to the reflected response.
preconditions: Application is running and the /api/comment/greet endpoint is reachable.
A browser or HTTP client capable of rendering the response as text/html.
Access to URL-encoding utilities for constructing the test payloads.
testSteps: Step 1: Send GET /api/comment/greet?name=Alice and confirm the response body contains 'Hello, Alice!' (positive baseline).

Step 3: Inspect the raw response body and confirm the literal characters '<script>alert('XSS')</script>' (or equivalent entity-encoded form) are present and that the raw characters '<script>' and '</script>' do NOT appear in the response.
Step 4: Send GET /api/comment/greet?name= and confirm the response contains '<img src=x onerror=alert(1)>' (or equivalent entity-encoded form) and the raw '

Step 5: Send GET /api/comment/greet?name="><svg/onload=alert(1)> and confirm the response contains the entity-encoded form ('"><svg/onload=alert(1)>' or equivalent) and the raw characters '"' followed by '>' are not present un-escaped.
Step 6: Render the response in a real browser and confirm no JavaScript alert/dialog fires.
Step 7: Send GET /api/comment/greet with no name parameter and confirm the response defaults to 'Hello, World!' (regression check for null handling).
Step 8: Check the response Content-Type header is text/html and not reflected into an executable context (e.g. not served as text/javascript).
testCases.expectedResult: The legitimate 'Hello, Alice!' behaviour is preserved. All HTML/JS metacharacters in the 'name' parameter are entity-encoded in the response, so the browser renders them as text and does not execute any injected script, image onerror, or SVG onload handler. No alert dialog fires when the page is rendered.
references: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
